cluster.title: Fun Relationship Games — Kefiw
cluster.subhead: Novelty compatibility tools — for laughs, not predictions.
cluster.intro: Three playful, transparent name-and-signal games: a letter-based love calculator, the classic FLAMES schoolyard game, and an 8-question relationship longevity quiz. Every result is deterministic — same inputs always return the same answer — and every result is explicitly for fun.
cluster.description: Love calculator, FLAMES name game, and a "how long will we last?" quiz. Deterministic, transparent, and explicitly novelty tools.

faq.a: No. They are novelty tools that use letter math or quiz answers to produce deterministic results. They are built for fun and conversation, not prediction.

No love calculator can predict real relationships. What letter-based tools measure is letter overlap; what signal-based quizzes measure is how you answered the quiz. Real compatibility depends on time, care, and daily choices.

FLAMES is a 1970s schoolyard game. Write two names, cross out shared letters, count what remains, and cycle through F-L-A-M-E-S (Friends, Lovers, Affectionate, Marriage, Enemies, Siblings) to land on one letter.

faq.q: Can you get 100% on a love calculator?
faq.a: Some calculators always return numbers near 100 for fun. Kefiw’s smooths its results to the 10–90 range to stay honest — a 100% score would undermine the "for fun" framing.

tools.title: Love Calculator — Name Compatibility for Fun

current.description: Enter two names and get a compatibility percentage based on shared letters, vowel balance, and the classic LOVES tally. For fun only.
current.intro: A deterministic novelty calculator. Enter two names and the tool scores them from three transparent signals: shared letters, vowel harmony, and a LOVES-letter tally. Same names always give the same score — swap nicknames or full names to see how it changes.
current.outcomeLine: Three transparent signals combined into a reproducible compatibility percentage.
howTo: Read the percentage and the verdict blurb.
Try nicknames and full legal names to see how the score shifts.

examples.title: Alex + Sam
examples.body: Limited letter overlap, similar vowel balance. Score usually lands in the mid-range.

examples.title: Jordan + Morgan
examples.body: More shared letters and common vowels push the score higher.

examples.title: Kit + Aurora
examples.body: Very different letter sets usually score lower — which can be read as complementary energies.

faq.a: No. It is a novelty tool. It combines letter overlap, vowel balance, and a LOVES tally into a reproducible percent. The formula is deterministic — it does not know the humans attached to the names.

faq.q: How does the love calculator work?
faq.a: It averages three signals: how many unique letters the names share, how close their vowel ratios are, and how many LOVES letters appear in the combined name. The result is smoothed to cluster between 10 and 90 percent.

faq.q: Can I get 100% on the love calculator?
faq.a: Not with this one — the formula caps the score around 90 to keep results honest and playful. Any calculator that always returns 100% is using the score for entertainment, not math.

faq.q: Does the love calculator predict marriage?
faq.a: No. A letter-based compatibility score cannot predict relationships. Treat it as a conversation starter — and use [How Long Will We Last?](/games/how-long-will-we-last/) for a signal-based quiz instead.

tools.title: FLAMES Calculator — The Classic Name Game

current.description: The classic 1970s schoolyard game — cross out shared letters from two names, count what remains, and cycle through FLAMES to find your result.
current.intro: FLAMES is a playground game from the 1970s: cross out shared letters in two names, count the remaining letters, and use that count to cycle through the letters F-L-A-M-E-S (Friends, Lovers, Affectionate, Marriage, Enemies, Siblings). The last letter standing is your result.
current.outcomeLine: Cross out shared letters, count the remainder, and cycle through FLAMES.
howTo: The tool crosses out matching letter pairs and shows which letter of FLAMES wins.

examples.title: Alex + Sam
examples.body: Shared: A. Remaining letters in ALEX: L, E, X; in SAM: S, M. Total remaining: 5 — cycles through FLAMES to Enemies.

examples.title: Pat + Mia
examples.body: Shared: A. Remaining: P, T; I, M — 4 letters. Cycles to Marriage.

faq.a: F = Friends, L = Lovers, A = Affectionate, M = Marriage, E = Enemies, S = Siblings. The letter that survives the elimination is your verdict.

faq.q: How does the FLAMES game work?
faq.a: Write both names. Cross out letters that appear in both (one for one). Count the letters remaining in both names combined. Use that number to cycle through F-L-A-M-E-S, removing a letter each time until only one remains.
No. FLAMES is a novelty game. It measures letter overlap, not compatibility. Treat the result as entertainment.

faq.q: Why do I keep getting Enemies?
faq.a: E sits in a spot that the elimination math hits often for short name pairs. Try full names or nicknames to see different results.

tools.title: How Long Will We Last? — Relationship Longevity Quiz

current.description: An 8-question novelty quiz that estimates years together from answers about values, conflict, trust, family, and shared life.
current.intro: An 8-question quiz that combines how you answer to produce an estimated years-together figure. Each answer contributes a transparent score; the tool shows which signals pushed the result up and which pulled it down. For fun — a conversation starter, not a prediction.
current.outcomeLine: Weighted scores across 8 signals → an estimated years-together figure and which signals matter most.
howTo: Answer the 8 questions about values, conflict, laughter, distance, future, family, and trust.
Read the estimated years and the strongest vs weakest signals.
Change an answer to see how the number shifts.

examples.title: Mostly positive answers
examples.body: Strong values alignment, low conflict, solid trust → often lands in the 20–40 years range.

examples.title: Mixed signals
examples.body: Good laughter and future talk, but long-distance and family tension → often lands in 3–8 years.

examples.title: Early days
examples.body: Known under 3 months, unknown values alignment, no cohabitation → often a short arc with weak signals.

faq.q: Can a quiz predict how long a relationship will last?
faq.a: No quiz can predict relationship longevity. Real outcomes depend on daily choices, timing, and life events a quiz cannot capture. Treat this as a reflection tool, not a forecast.

faq.q: What does the years-together number mean?
faq.a: It is a deterministic mapping from your 8 answers to an estimated range. Strong positive answers push toward decades; weak or negative answers push toward months. Changing one answer shifts the number predictably.
Values alignment, conflict style, and trust tend to dominate real research on relationship longevity. This quiz weights every question equally, so results are sensitive to any single weak signal.

faq.q: Is this a real compatibility test?
faq.a: No. Real compatibility assessments used in therapy and research are far more detailed. Use the quiz for fun, then talk about the weakest signal together.

enhancement_brief.goal: Lift each article from short-form summary to authority-grade long-form. Target 1,200+ words per article while keeping the structured fields crisp.
per_article_outputs: 1. Fill `enhanced` with any structured-field overrides (usually: tighter intro, expanded keyPoints, more examples, richer FAQ).
2. Fill `longform_markdown` with 800-1200 word prose body (markdown, H2 sections only — no H1).
3. Do not modify `current` — that is the baseline snapshot for diffing.

rules: Each question must read like a real search query — not marketing-speak.
First sentence of answer is a complete standalone 15-25 word definition/answer (Google quotes it directly).
Full answer 40-60 words. Tight. Front-load the answer.
Cover distinct intents: definition, comparison, edge-case, how-to, troubleshooting. Not 3 rewordings of the same angle.
Include one target keyword in at least half the questions.
Add a faq_intent tag per entry: "definition" | "comparison" | "edge-case" | "how-to" | "troubleshooting" | "cost" | "trust"
faq_rules.target_count: 5-7 entries per article, 4-6 per tool page

structure: ## H2 section 1 — deeper "what is it" / definition with context
## H2 section 2 — the mechanic / how it works (with one small example)
## H2 section 3 — when to use vs when to avoid
## H2 section 4 — worked example or walkthrough
## H2 section 6 — tool-specific tips (link out to the tool)
longform_rules.tone: Concrete, useful, first-person-plural light. No filler. No "in conclusion". Short paragraphs.
links: Every time you mention a sibling tool in this cluster, link it with the exact URL from this brief (tools[].url).
Every time you mention a sibling article, link it with the exact URL from this brief (articles[].url).
Do not invent URLs or link to external competitors.
forbidden: Medical/legal/financial advice language ("you should", "we recommend") — use neutral framing.
Generic SEO boilerplate ("in today's fast-paced world").

internal_linking.rule: Use only URLs that appear in this JSON. Prefer linking to sibling tools/articles in THIS cluster over outbound links.

quality_gates: No fabricated facts — if uncertain, phrase as "in practice" or remove.
Every H2 section earns its space — cut if it restates the intro.
Zero AI-tells: "it is important to note", "in conclusion", "let us delve", "furthermore".
